---
title: Execution
description: The executable semantics of a studyflow - the tasks, the scoped-token model, and the rules
order: 8
---

The [Specification](spec.qmd) says what a studyflow *is*. This page says what it *does*: the meaning of each element as an operation an engine performs, precise enough that two independent engines running the same `.studyflow` produce the same run.

The design rests on one observation.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" title="The unification"}
A participant walking through a study, a fold in cross-validation, a point in a hyperparameter grid, a shard of an HPC array job, and a turn of an agent loop are the same thing: **a token in a scope**.

:::

## The primitives

The whole executable language is a small set of primitives, each an existing BPMN construct given a run-time meaning by the `exec` schema. This table *is* the language; everything domain-specific — a `Split`, a `Fit`, a `Retriever`, a `Judge` — is a **template** that binds a function onto one of these via [`uses`](authoring-schemas.qmd), not a type of its own.

| Primitive | BPMN construct | `exec` element | Domain-specific things are… |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| typed **step** | activity + data associations | `Step` trait | a template binding `uses` (fit, evaluate, retrieve, judge) |
| **artifact** with lineage | data object / store | `Artifact` trait | `ml:Model`, `ml:Metric`, `agentic:Prompt` |
| **fan-out / sweep** | multi-instance marker | `ForEach`, `Sweep` | cross-validation, hyperparameter search |
| **loop** | standard-loop marker | `Repeat` | evaluator-optimizer, retrain-on-drift |
| **conditional gate** | exclusive gateway | `Gate` | a promotion gate |
| **model-driven branch** | exclusive gateway | `agentic:Router` | — (irreducible) |
| **model-driven sub-process** | ad-hoc sub-process | `agentic:Agent` | — (irreducible) |
| **reusable component** | call activity | `Component` | a published sub-flow |
| **config composition** | data object | `Parameters` | a study or experiment variant |
| **human-in-the-loop** | user task | `agentic:HumanApproval` | feedback, labeling, preference collection |

Two rows are genuinely new and cannot be a template — `Router` and `Agent` — because they change *who decides control flow*. The rest of the AI/ML surface is a binding of a row above. The reasoning behind that split is in [`exec_schema_review.md`](exec_schema_review.md).

## The step contract

An activity is executable when it answers six questions. Two are already part of the language ([`uses` and `with`](authoring-schemas.qmd)); the `exec` schema adds the rest.

| Question | Carried by |
| --- | --- |
| What runs? | `uses` — `python://`, `docker://`, `https://`, `file://` |
| With what arguments? | `with` — a YAML mapping |
| Over what interface? | the data associations drawn on the canvas — native BPMN, nothing declared |
| Where does it run? | `environment` |
| May it be skipped? | `when` — a guard over the scope's bindings; false means skip |
| What if it fails? | any attached boundary event |

This applies to *every* activity — a cognitive task, a data transform, a model fit, an agent. A `cognitive:CognitiveTask` is a step whose `environment` is `browser`.

## Scopes and tokens

A **token** is a triple $\tau = \langle n, \sigma, \beta \rangle$: the node it sits at, the scope it lives in, and its bindings (port name $\rightarrow$ artifact digest).

A **scope** $\sigma = \langle f_1, \dots, f_k \rangle$ is a stack of frames. A frame is a `(kind, key, value)` triple, and the kinds are exactly the ways a run can widen:

| Frame kind | Opened by | One instance per |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `participant` | a cohort run | subject |
| `item` | `exec:ForEach` | element of a collection |
| `shard` | `exec:ForEach`, `iterate: shards` | shard of the input |
| `fold` | `iterate: folds` | cross-validation fold |
| `config` | `exec:Sweep` | point of the sweep space |
| `turn` | `exec:Repeat`, `agentic:Agent` | iteration of a loop |
| `retry` | a failed attempt | attempt |

Two tokens in different scopes never join. A binding made in $\sigma$ is visible in $\sigma$ and in every extension $\sigma \cdot f$, and a collector writes back into the parent.

## Variables

The Study/Experiment is the root scope for variables. Each frame adds a new layer of variables, and the language defines a few that are always present:

| Variable | Value |
| --- | --- |
| `seed` | a deterministic integer derived from the root seed and the scope; used for random branching and model-driven routing |
| `scope` | the current scope $\sigma$ |


## Firing rules

A run configuration is $\langle T, A, E, W \rangle$: a multiset of tokens, a content-addressed artifact store, an append-only execution log, and a set of suspended tokens. The log $E$ *is* the run — everything else can be rebuilt from it.

**[Seq]** A token at a non-gateway node with one outgoing flow moves along it, keeping its scope.

**[Branch]** At a gateway, the branch is chosen by the type's `meta.branching`:

- `condition` — evaluate the outgoing `conditionExpression`s in $(\sigma, \beta)$; take the first that holds, else the default. (`exec:Gate`, plain exclusive gateways.)
- `random` — draw from $\text{seed}(\sigma)$. (`cognitive:RandomGateway`, `cognitive:StratifiedAllocationGateway`.)
- `model` — a classifier picks. (`agentic:Router`.)

In all three cases the chosen branch is written to $E$. A replay reads the recorded branch; it does not re-draw or re-ask.

**[Fork] / [Join]** A parallel gateway emits one token per outgoing flow in the same scope; the join consumes one token per incoming flow *with equal scope* and merges their bindings. Scope equality is what makes the join safe under fan-out: two tokens from different folds cannot accidentally synchronize.

**[Expand]** An activity carrying a multi-instance marker computes its instance set $I$ from `iterate` — the collection named by `over`, the folds in `folds`, the points of `grid`, the shard count in `compute.array` — and emits one token per $i \in I$ at scope $\sigma \cdot (\textit{kind}, \textit{key}, i)$. With `sequential`, one at a time; otherwise up to `maxConcurrency` at once.

**[Collect]** When every instance reaches the body's end, their outputs are joined per `collect` (`list`, `concat`, `reduce` with `reducer`, or `none`), bound to `collectAs` in the parent scope, and a single token continues.

**[Loop]** An activity carrying a standard-loop marker re-enters its body at scope $\sigma \cdot (\textit{turn}, n, k{+}1)$ while `until` fails to hold and $k <$ `maxTurns`. On exhaustion, `onMaxTurns` decides between falling through and failing. **Always set `maxTurns`** — it is the only guaranteed termination bound in the language.

**[Skip]** A step carrying a `when` guard that does not hold in $(\sigma, \beta)$ does not fire: the skip is written to $E$, its outputs stay unbound, and the token moves on. The step-level form of a `Gate`, for skips that do not deserve a branch on the diagram.

**[Fire]** A step whose required input ports are all bound computes its cache key

$$\kappa = H\big(\, n.\text{id},\; \text{digest}(\texttt{uses}),\; \texttt{with}{\downarrow}\sigma,\; \{\text{digest}(a) : a \in \text{inputs}\},\; \text{seed}(\sigma) \,\big)$$

and then:

- if `cache: reuse` and $E$ holds a successful execution with key $\kappa$ — **rebind its outputs and skip**;
- otherwise dispatch to the executor for `runsOn`, append an `Execution` record to $E$, and register the outputs in $A$.

`digest(uses)` is why the [`uses` grammar](authoring-schemas.qmd) carries `@version-or-digest`: `python://pkg.fn@1.2` keys on the version, `docker://img@sha256:…` on the digest, `https://…` on the hash of the fetched script. A step whose code moved is a step whose key moved, so it re-runs. This is `dvc repro` and Flyte caching, obtained from a field the language already had.

**[Suspend] / [Resume]** A dispatch may return a wait handle instead of a result. The token parks in $W$, the run is checkpointed, and **the worker may exit**. An external event carrying the handle resumes it.

This one rule covers a human approval that takes three days, a Slurm job that takes six hours, and a participant who closes the tab and comes back tomorrow. The browser runner has always suspended on every cognitive task while it waits for a person to respond; the only new thing is that a machine step can suspend too.

## Determinism and replay

A step declares whether it is a pure function of its inputs, arguments, and seed. Steps that are not — a human response, an LLM call above temperature 0, a wall-clock read, a model-driven route — set `deterministic: false`, and the engine **records their outcome in $E$ rather than assuming it can re-derive it**.

A run is therefore replayable even though it is not deterministic: a replay reads recorded outcomes instead of re-drawing them. This is event-sourced durable execution, and it is what makes an agentic run debuggable — you can step through what the model actually did, not what it would probably do again.

## Runtime targets

`environment` selects an executor; it never changes what a step means. The same `ml:Operation` bound to a fit function is the same operation whether it runs in a worker process, a container, or a Slurm array job.

| `runsOn` | Executor |
| --- | --- |
| `browser` | the runner tab; React node components |
| `local` | the worker's own process |
| `container` | an OCI image named by `uses` |
| `hpc` | submitted to `scheduler` with `compute`; **suspends** until the job reports |
| `cloud` | a hosted queue |

`inherit` (the default) resolves against the enclosing sub-process and finally `studyflow:Study#runtime`. Scaling out is a change of `runsOn` and `compute`, not a change of diagram.

## Agents

BPMN defines an **ad-hoc sub-process** as one whose contained activities execute *in an order determined at run time, rather than by the diagram*. That is, in the spec's own words, the distinction between an agent and a workflow. The element has been in BPMN since 2011.

So `agentic:Agent` is a `bpmn:AdHocSubProcess`, and:

- **the tools are the activities drawn inside it** — containment is the tool list;
- **a tool is an ordinary step** — same `uses`, same wired data elements, same log entry, so anything a studyflow can run, an agent can call;
- **the turn loop is `exec:Repetition`** — `until` and `maxTurns`, the same construct as any other loop;
- **`deterministic` is pinned false** — every model call and tool call lands in $E$.

Everything *outside* an Agent follows a path the diagram fixes. Inside one, nothing does. Keeping that boundary visible is the point of drawing it: a reader can see at a glance which parts of a system are steered by a model.

## The interface

A step's interface is not declared anywhere — it is *drawn*, and it is *reused*:

- **Wiring is BPMN's own data association.** An input is a data element wired in by a `dataInputAssociation`; an output is one wired out by a `dataOutputAssociation`. The engine reads the associations the diagram already has; there is no parallel port declaration to keep in sync.
- **Types live on the data elements, once.** `Table`, `Dataset`, `Timeseries`, `ml:Model`, `ml:Metric`, `agentic:Prompt`, `Blob` are element types the language already has — the type of a port is the type of the element on the other end of the wire. Schema conformance is the data element's own business (a `Dataset` names its [`studyflow:Schema`](data.qmd)); a step never re-asserts it.
- **The callable's parameter list is the fine-grained contract.** `python://sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier` already declares what it accepts; the notation does not re-declare a function's own signature. `with` supplies arguments; wired elements and scope bindings supply data, matched by name.
- **Scope plumbing is by name.** Values that never earn a canvas element (a fold's metrics, a loop's `scores`) travel as scope bindings under the names `collectAs`, `itemVar`, and the step's outputs give them. The core `inputs`/`outputs` string lists remain for declaring these explicitly when it helps a reader.

This is the reuse rule applied to the interface itself: BPMN defines the wiring, the existing artifact types define the typing, and the callable defines the parameters. Nothing was added — an earlier `signature` YAML block that re-declared all three was cut.

## Fitting

Everything that learns is fitted by the same step, and the shape is drawn, not declared: a **trainable artifact** (`Model` or `Prompt`) wired in and back out by data associations — same element, new digest, `producedBy` recording the fit — **data** wired in, and `Metric`s out, each with a `goal` declaring which way is better.

One shape covers what five ecosystems spell differently — every row is a template binding a function onto `ml:Operation`, not a class:

| The fit | Trainable | Data | Objective | Template |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| classical estimator | `Model` | `Table` | the estimator's loss | `Fit model` (scikit-learn) |
| statistical formula | `Model` | `Table` | likelihood | `Fit formula` — `rt ~ condition + (1 \| participant_id)` |
| fine-tune (SFT / LoRA) | `Model` (adapter) | `Dataset` | next-token loss | `Fine-tune` |
| preference tuning | `Model` | preference `Table` | human preferences | `Preference-tune` (DPO) |
| prompt optimization | `Prompt` | scored examples | a judge's `Metric` | `Optimize prompt` (DSPy-style compile) |

Three consequences:

- **A prompt is fitted like a model.** `agentic:Prompt` carries `digest`, `version`, and `producedBy` from the `Artifact` trait, so a compiled prompt has the same lineage as a fitted model — which execution produced it, from what data. Prompt lineage is model lineage.
- **The objective can be a measurement the flow itself makes.** An LLM-as-judge or a human rating produces the `Metric` an optimizer maximizes — and the preference pairs DPO fits on are collected by `Pairwise preference`, whose raters can be the study's own participants. Collection, fitting, and serving are one diagram.
- **The loops around fitting are the `exec` constructs, unchanged.** A hyperparameter search is a `Sweep` around a fitting step; cross-validation is `iterate: folds`; compile-until-it-clears is a `Repeat` whose `until` reads the eval's metric (see `agent_eval.studyflow`, where the optimizer is `when`-guarded to skip the first turn so the hand-written prompt gets a baseline); retrain-on-drift is a boundary signal routed back into the fit. The evaluator-optimizer is the degenerate case: the same `Repeat`, with the response rather than an artifact as the thing improved.

## One meaning, two spellings: the native BPMN mapping

Several `exec` fields name things BPMN 2.0 already has a construct for. That is deliberate — the field is the *compact authoring spelling*, the native construct is the *wire and interop form* — but it is only harmless if the equivalence is declared once and mirrored mechanically (the Stage 2 mirror), so no field carries a second meaning. This table is that declaration.

| Studyflow field | Native BPMN construct | Note |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `until` | `standardLoopCharacteristics/loopCondition` | **negated** — BPMN loops *while* its condition holds; `until` stops when it does |
| `until` (on an `Agent`) | `adHocSubProcess/completionCondition` | BPMN's own exit test for "order decided at run time" |
| `maxTurns` | `loopMaximum` | identical |
| `testBefore` | `testBefore` | identical, name for name |
| `sequential` | `multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics/isSequential` | identical |
| `over` / `itemVar` / `collectAs` | `loopDataInputRef` / `inputDataItem` / `loopDataOutputRef` | identical roles |
| shard count (`compute.array`) | `loopCardinality` | instance count as an expression |
| `Gate.criterion` | the pass flow's `conditionExpression` | the engine's [Branch] rule reads the flow; `criterion` is mirrored onto it |
| Router fallback branch | the gateway's `default` flow | native; the Router has no fallback field |
| `timeout` | a boundary timer's `timeDuration` | draw the boundary event when the timeout path deserves to be visible |
| `retry` | error boundary event + re-entry | BPMN has no retry count; the prop is the compact form |
| `Component.source` | `Definitions/imports` + `calledElement` | a registry URL in one field instead of an import block |
| step interface | `dataInputAssociation` / `dataOutputAssociation` | fully native — nothing custom; a `signature` block that re-declared the wiring was cut |
| `approvers` | `resources` → `PotentialOwner` | a name list instead of the resource-role machinery |
| `iterate: folds` / `grid`, `collect: reduce`, `seed`, `cache`, `when`, `deterministic` | — | genuinely new; no native construct exists |

Everything in the left column that has a right column is *one* semantic with two spellings. Everything marked "genuinely new" is the actual extension surface — deliberately small.

## Provenance: the as-run record

A `.studyflow` file is a **plan** — `prov:Plan`, precisely. Running it produces a `.studyrun`: the same document, plus what actually happened.

```yaml
studyflow: sha256:9f2c…          # pins the exact plan
run: 2026-07-14T09:12:03Z/a41f
rootSeed: 42
executions:
  - key: sha256:1b7e…
    node: Fit_Point
    scope: {config: {max_depth: 8, n_estimators: 500}}
    actor: {id: slurm://cluster/job/88213, type: agent}
    used: [sha256:c31a…]          # prov:used
    generated: [sha256:77de…]     # prov:wasGeneratedBy
    metrics: {roc_auc: 0.83}
    status: ok
artifacts:
  sha256:77de…: {type: Model, uri: s3://models/…, producedBy: sha256:1b7e…}
```

The mapping to [W3C PROV](https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-o/) is direct: an execution is a `prov:Activity`, an artifact a `prov:Entity`, a `cognitive:Actor` a `prov:Agent`, and the plan is `prov:hadPlan`. The prospective/retrospective distinction the scientific-workflow community formalized decades ago is the one Studyflow already draws between a spec and its data.

Studyflow has done this in miniature all along: [`EndEvent#attritionCount`](elements.qmd) is left empty at registration and written by the runner as participants exit, so the diff between the registered file and the as-run file *is* the CONSORT attrition table. The as-run record generalizes that — the diff between plan and run is the result.

## What the reference runner implements today

The schema describes the full semantics; the browser runner implements the part a browser can. Being explicit about the gap:

| | Status |
| --- | --- |
| Sequential walk, exclusive/random branching, condition expressions | implemented |
| Human suspension (participant tasks) | implemented, on every cognitive task |
| `uses` / `with` parsed and validated | implemented; **not executed** |
| Parallel gateway, sub-process traversal | **not supported** — the runner refuses rather than guessing |
| Model-driven routing (`agentic:Router`) | **not supported** — the runner refuses rather than silently taking the default branch |
| Scopes, expansion, collection, caching, skip guards (`when`), the as-run record | **not implemented** |

Refusing is deliberate. A runner that silently took the default branch at a `Router` would produce a run that looks fine and means nothing.
